transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Linda. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about my future and what I should do with my job. It’s just that every time I talk to my parents about it, I feel this pressure. They have so many expectations, and I just... I don’t know if I can meet them. It’s kind of overwhelming.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling a lot of pressure from your parents regarding your job and future, and that can be really overwhelming. You mentioned thinking a lot about your future—what kind of thoughts or feelings come up for you when you consider your options?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... when I think about changing jobs, I start to feel anxious. I keep telling myself I’m not capable of doing something better, and honestly, the idea of putting in a lot of effort is really daunting. I wish my family would just understand where I’m coming from and be more supportive. It feels like I’m stuck between wanting to make them happy and figuring out what I really want for myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're caught in a tough spot between your own desires and the expectations from your family. The anxiety you feel when thinking about changing jobs seems to be tied to a belief that you might not be capable of doing something better. That must be really challenging. Can you tell me more about where that belief comes from? Have there been experiences in your past that contribute to feeling this way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess... I’ve always felt like I’m not quite good enough, you know? It’s like, whenever I try something new, there’s this voice in my head that says I won’t succeed. I can’t really pinpoint where it all started, but I think it’s just built up over time. My parents have always pushed me to do well, and while I appreciate their support, it sometimes feels like they’re setting the bar too high. I don’t want to disappoint them, but at the same time, I’m scared of failing. It’s just a lot to handle.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that feeling of not being good enough has been a long-standing struggle for you, and it’s tied to the high expectations from your parents. It’s understandable that you would feel scared of failing, especially when you also want to make them proud. The pressure to meet those expectations can be really heavy. When you hear that voice in your head telling you that you won’t succeed, how does that affect your motivation or willingness to try new things?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It definitely makes it harder to even consider trying something new. I find myself hesitating a lot, like I’ll think about applying for a different job or taking on a new project, but then that voice just shuts me down. I start to feel overwhelmed and anxious, and I end up convincing myself it’s not worth the effort. It’s frustrating because I know I have potential, but that fear just keeps me stuck. I wish I could shake it off and feel more confident about taking risks.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really frustrating to feel that potential within you but be held back by that fear and anxiety. It’s like there’s a part of you that wants to take risks and explore new opportunities, but that voice makes it feel overwhelming. When you think about shaking off that fear and feeling more confident, what do you imagine that would look like for you? What would it feel like to take those risks?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think if I could shake off that fear, it would feel... liberating, maybe? Like I could finally breathe and explore what I really want without constantly second-guessing myself. I imagine being able to go for that job I’ve been eyeing or even just having a conversation with my parents about my choices without feeling so anxious. It would be nice to feel proud of my decisions instead of ashamed or embarrassed. I guess I just want to feel like I’m in control of my own life, rather than being pulled in different directions by everyone else’s expectations.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sense of liberation and control sounds really important to you. It seems like you’re yearning for the freedom to explore your own desires and make choices that feel right for you, rather than being weighed down by others’ expectations. Feeling proud of your decisions instead of ashamed is a powerful aspiration. What do you think might be the first step toward feeling more in control and taking those risks?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe the first step could be just acknowledging my own feelings and recognizing that it’s okay to want something different. I often get so caught up in what my family thinks that I forget to check in with myself. Maybe I could start by setting small goals—like researching job options or even just talking to someone who’s in a field I’m interested in. It feels a bit scary, but I think if I can take those small steps, it might help build my confidence. I just need to remind myself that it’s okay to take things at my own pace.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Acknowledging your feelings and giving yourself permission to want something different sounds like a really meaningful first step. It’s great that you’re considering setting small goals, like researching job options or reaching out to someone in a field you’re interested in. Taking things at your own pace can help you build that confidence gradually. How do you feel about starting with those small steps? What support might you need to help you along the way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit nervous about starting those small steps, but also a little hopeful. I think having some support would really help me, like maybe talking to a friend who understands what I’m going through. Just having someone to bounce ideas off of or to encourage me could make a difference. I also think it would help to remind myself that it’s okay to take my time and that I don’t have to have everything figured out right away. It’s just hard sometimes to break out of that mindset of feeling like I need to have it all together.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel nervous yet hopeful about taking those small steps. Having a supportive friend to talk to and share your thoughts with can make a big difference. It sounds like you’re recognizing the importance of being gentle with yourself and allowing time for this process, which is really valuable. Breaking out of that mindset of needing to have everything figured out can be challenging, but it’s a significant part of finding your own path. What do you think might help you stay focused on that gentler approach as you begin to take these steps?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe setting reminders for myself could help, like jotting down positive affirmations or things I want to achieve. It might also be useful to keep a journal to track my progress and how I’m feeling about everything. That way, I can see how far I’ve come, even if the steps feel small. I guess having a plan to check in with myself regularly could help me stay focused on being gentle and patient with my journey. It’s just about finding ways to keep that voice of doubt at bay, I suppose.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like wonderful strategies! Setting reminders and jotting down positive affirmations can really help reinforce that gentler approach you want to take. Keeping a journal to track your progress and feelings is a great way to reflect on your journey and celebrate even the small steps. It seems like you’re finding ways to create a supportive environment for yourself, which is really important. How do you feel about starting with one of these strategies this week? Which one resonates with you the most right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think starting with the journal feels like the best option for me right now. It seems like a safe space to express my thoughts and feelings without judgment. I can write about my progress and how I’m feeling as I take those small steps. Plus, it might help me process everything a bit better. I’m a little nervous about it, but I think it could be really beneficial. I’ll try to make it a regular part of my week and see how it goes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Starting with the journal sounds like a great choice, especially since it offers you a safe space to express yourself and process your thoughts and feelings. It’s completely normal to feel a bit nervous about it, but it also seems like you’re open to the potential benefits it could bring. Making it a regular part of your week can help establish a routine that supports your journey. As you begin this practice, what do you think might be the most important thing to keep in mind to help you stay committed to it?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the most important thing to keep in mind is to be kind to myself and not to put too much pressure on how I should be writing or what I should be feeling. It’s okay if some days I don’t have much to say or if I feel stuck. I just want to remind myself that it’s a process, and it’s okay to take it one step at a time. I want to focus on being honest with myself in the journal, even if it feels uncomfortable sometimes. That way, I can really use it as a tool for growth.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a beautiful mindset to have as you start your journaling practice. Being kind to yourself and allowing for the ebb and flow of your thoughts and feelings is essential for growth. It’s great that you want to focus on honesty, even when it feels uncomfortable, as that can lead to deeper insights. Remember, it’s all part of the process, and taking it one step at a time is perfectly okay. How do you feel about checking in with yourself after a week of journaling to see how it’s going?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think checking in after a week sounds like a good idea. It’ll give me a chance to reflect on how the journaling is going and see if it’s helping me feel more in control or if there are any challenges I need to address. I’m a little anxious about what I might discover, but I also think it could be really helpful to see my progress. I’ll make a note to remind myself to do that. It feels like a good way to keep myself accountable while also being gentle with my journey.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful plan, Linda. Checking in after a week will give you an opportunity to reflect on your experience and any insights you gain from journaling. It’s completely normal to feel anxious about what you might discover, but it also sounds like you’re approaching this with a sense of curiosity and a desire for growth. Keeping yourself accountable while being gentle with your journey is a wonderful balance. If you ever feel the need to talk about your reflections or any challenges that come up, I’m here to support you. How are you feeling about everything we’ve discussed today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of relief and hope, to be honest. It’s nice to have a plan and some strategies to work with, and I appreciate having a space to talk about everything. I still have some anxiety about taking those steps, but I also feel a bit more empowered now that I have a clearer idea of what I want to do. I think the journaling will help me process things, and knowing that I can reach out for support if I need it is comforting. Overall, I feel like I’m starting to take control of my situation, even if it’s just a little at a time. Thank you for listening and guiding me through this.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you’re feeling a mix of relief and hope. It’s wonderful that you have a plan and strategies to work with, and I appreciate you sharing your thoughts and feelings with me. Taking control of your situation, even in small steps, is a significant achievement. Remember, it’s okay to feel anxious along the way; those feelings are part of the process. I’m here to support you as you navigate this journey. If you ever want to talk again or share your reflections after journaling, don’t hesitate to reach out. You’re doing great, Linda.

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much for your support. It really means a lot to me. I’ll definitely keep in mind that I can reach out if I need to talk again. I appreciate the encouragement, and I’m looking forward to seeing how the journaling goes. It feels good to have someone in my corner. I’ll take it one step at a time, and hopefully, I’ll start to feel more confident along the way. Thank you again for everything!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Linda. I’m glad to hear that you feel supported, and I’m here for you as you take those steps forward. It’s great that you’re focusing on one step at a time, and I believe you’ll find your confidence growing along the way. Remember to be kind to yourself throughout this journey. I wish you the best with your journaling, and I look forward to hearing about your progress whenever you’re ready to share. Take care, and know that you’re not alone in this!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I really appreciate your kind words and support. It helps to know that I’m not alone in this. I’ll keep working on my journaling and take those steps at my own pace. I’ll definitely share my progress when I feel ready. Take care, and thanks again for everything!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Linda! I’m glad to hear that you feel supported and that you’re committed to your journaling and taking things at your own pace. I look forward to hearing about your progress whenever you feel ready to share. Take care of yourself, and remember to be gentle with your journey. Wishing you all the best as you move forward!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much! I really appreciate your encouragement and understanding. I’ll keep that in mind as I move forward. It feels good to have a plan and a little bit of hope. I’ll take care of myself and look forward to our next conversation. Take care!
